Scenario.title: Spearphishing Attack on Communications Network
Scenario.overview: Communications Simple This scenario outlines a basic spearphishing campaign targeting organizations in the communications industry, resulting in unauthorized service execution and data exfiltration.
Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Internet-facing assets, Windows Service, File
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Delivery Method, Service Execution, File Access (Mechanism)

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.description: The attacker initiates contact by sending spearphishing emails that appear to originate from legitimate services within the communications sector, such as billing or customer support platforms hosted on internet-facing assets. These emails exploit social engineering techniques to deceive recipients into opening attachments or clicking links, thereby providing a delivery method for malicious payloads designed to gain unauthorized access.

stage.name: Execution
stage.description: Upon successful exploitation during initial access, the attacker executes a malicious script embedded in a document (e.g., Microsoft Word or Excel file) that interacts with the Windows service architecture. This allows the installation and activation of unauthorized services, which run automatically at system startup, enabling further command execution and establishing a foothold in the compromised environment.

stage.name: Impact
stage.description: The adversary leverages the established access to steal sensitive data directly from local system files, such as configuration databases or log archives relevant to communication networks. This involves querying or extracting information through file access mechanisms, potentially disrupting normal operations or facilitating espionage activities by compromising confidentiality.

Scenario.summary: This simple spearphishing campaign against communications firms achieves basic impact through targeted deception, automated service execution, and local data theft, demonstrating low sophistication yet effective exploitation of common vulnerabilities.
